title: Arts - The Kathmandu Post
summary: Arts ‘Like a Rising River’ explores Nepali women through art By Mokshyada Thapa Fifteen artists translate lived experiences from across Nepal into powerful visual narratives on labour, identity, and resilience. A decade of work on display in ‘Contours of Two’ By Post Report Artists Kapil Mani Dixit and Roshan Bhandari reflect on memory and nostalgia. Capturing the past and future in the same frame
content:
Arts ‘Like a Rising River’ explores Nepali women through art By Mokshyada Thapa Fifteen artists translate lived experiences from across Nepal into powerful visual narratives on labour, identity, and resilience. A decade of work on display in ‘Contours of Two’ By Post Report Artists Kapil Mani Dixit and Roshan Bhandari reflect on memory and nostalgia. Capturing the past and future in the same frame By Sanskriti Pokharel The exhibition ‘Changing Times’ suggests that the past and future are not opposites, but forces that continue to shape each other in the present. Botanical painting classes at Kathmandu Art Gallery By Post Report The workshop, being conducted at the gallery’s premises, runs from December 16 to January 18, 2026. From Venice to Kathmandu: Four decades through an artist’s eyes By Britta Gfeller Italian artist Giovanna Caruso’s exhibition at Kalā Salon reveals a deeply personal visual archive of festivals, faces and fleeting moments that trace her long relationship with Nepal. ‘Heritage Re-Imagined’ at Classic Gallery By Post Report The exhibit features works by 14 emerging artists selected through an open call. Takpa Gallery to unveil multidisciplinary exhibition By Post Report ‘Birdsong for the Confined’ by Shailee Bajracharya explores introspection, confinement and the transformative power of art. Narrating history through devotional art By Aarya Chand Jigme Tamang Mugum’s artworks at Gallery 108 show the lineage of Karmapas and their forebears as a visual cosmology. I want my life to have the same freedom as my brushstrokes By Sanskriti Pokharel After being bullied at school and feeling lost in the corporate world, Anna Dhakal found her true calling in painting. ‘Burned and Being’ opens at Dalai-la Art Space By Post Report The exhibit invites viewers to engage closely with the ceramics. Bringing unnoticed stories to life on paper By Anish Ghimire From chicken coops to old Patan houses, Pragya Mainali’s live sketches open a window to the beauty of everyday life. Nepal’s art residencies are drawing the world together By Reeva Khanal These residencies are turning Nepal’s galleries into crossroads for creativity—where artists from around the world immerse themselves in local heritage, and exchange ideas with Nepali peers. Manish Dhoju’s hyperrealist works on display By Post Report ‘Divine Realm’ showcases thirteen pieces created using graphite and charcoal on paper. Tharu printmaking finds life in rituals By Aarya Chand For generations, the Tharu have practised a quiet, enduring form of printmaking—one rooted not in studios, but in kitchens, courtyards, and rituals. ‘Yala Yatra’ at Gallery Mcube By Post Report American artist Peter Makela presents works inspired by sacred walks and worship in the Kathmandu Valley. Bringing Dolpo to canvas By Post Report At the Kalä Salon in Thamel, 34 artworks rooted in Dolpo’s culture and everyday life are on display. Examining human-nature ties By Aarya Chand The group exhibit ‘Interdependence’ gathers Nepali women artists to reflect on our fragile relationship with nature’s five elements—earth, water, fire, air, and space. Art exhibit questions animal sacrifice By Post Report Through powerful imagery, the Purna Viram Collective invites viewers to reflect on the brutal reality of the Gadhimai festival. ‘Kalāko Vansha: Exhibit 2’ opens at Gallery 108 By Post Report The exhibition showcases 108 Lokeshwaras sketches by the late Siddhi Muni Shakya. From nature to silence By Aarya Chand Min Thapa’s work explores the tension between nature and human-made change through haunting imagery and symbolic details.

The exhibition ‘Changing Times’ suggests that the past and future are not opposites, but forces that continue to shape each other in the present.

By Aarya Chand

The group exhibit ‘Interdependence’ gathers Nepali women artists to reflect on our fragile relationship with nature’s five elements—earth, water, fire, air, and space.

By Post Report

Through powerful imagery, the Purna Viram Collective invites viewers to reflect on the brutal reality of the Gadhimai festival.

By Post Report

Min Thapa’s work explores the tension between nature and human-made change through haunting imagery and symbolic details.
